name: US Bankruptcy Court East District
description_short: Federal bankruptcy court serving Eastern New York covering five counties. Provides case management, filing services, and judicial resolution for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcies.
description_long:
The United States Bankruptcy Court for the Eastern District of New York is a federal judicial unit operating under the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of New York. Established to serve the five counties of Richmond, Kings, Queens, Nassau, and Suffolk, this court handles bankruptcy proceedings across a major metropolitan area. Chief Judge Alan S. Trust leads the court with Paul Dickson serving as Clerk of Court.

The court offers comprehensive bankruptcy case management and filing services including electronic case filing through CM/ECF, case location via PACER, proof of claim filing through ePOC, and debtor electronic noticing. They provide dedicated support for both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 filings, including pro se (self-representation) filing options through their eSR program, fee waiver procedures, and means testing guidance. The court maintains two physical locations in Brooklyn and Central Islip with remote hearing capabilities via Zoom and Webex.

This court distinguishes itself through robust technology infrastructure including remote hearing registration via eCourt Appearances, electronic debtor education certificate filing (eFinCert), and enhanced cybersecurity procedures for highly sensitive documents per Administrative Order No. 716. They provide free financial literacy resources, loss mitigation services, mediation options, and dedicated support for pro se filers who cannot afford attorneys. The court also operates a C.A.R.E. (Credit Abuse Resistance Education) Program and maintains clear appeals processes and written opinions.

As a federal court, this is a government institution rather than a consumer finance service provider. It serves debtors and creditors impartially under Title 11 U.S. Bankruptcy Code with a stated mission to provide debtors a fresh economic start while ensuring creditors are paid according to law. Consumers should understand this is the actual court venue, not a bankruptcy attorney or filing service—they must either hire representation or file pro se through this court's systems.
pros: Free pro se filing option through eSR Electronic Self-Representation program for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13
Multiple remote hearing options via Zoom and Cisco Webex to reduce need for in-person attendance
Chapter 7 fee waiver procedures available for debtors who cannot afford standard filing fees
Free financial literacy and credit counseling resources through C.A.R.E. program
Two physical court locations (Brooklyn and Central Islip) with comprehensive accessibility
Electronic filing and case tracking through PACER and CM/ECF eliminates paper-based delays
Loss mitigation services and mediation options to resolve disputes outside courtroom proceedings
cons: As a federal court, this is not a service provider—consumers must navigate complex bankruptcy law themselves or hire private attorneys
Pro se filing carries significant legal risk; bankruptcy law is complex and mistakes can result in case dismissal or loss of protections
No guidance on affording bankruptcy attorney representation; the court assumes pro se filers have legal capacity
Remote hearing technology requirements (internet, device, ZoomGov/Webex access) may exclude some debtors
Court locations limited to Brooklyn and Central Islip; some residents in the five-county district face travel burden
best_for: Debtors in Eastern New York (Richmond, Kings, Queens, Nassau, Suffolk counties) filing Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 bankruptcy
Self-represented bankruptcy filers (pro se) who cannot afford attorney representation and meet fee waiver criteria
Creditors seeking to file claims or monitor cases through PACER and participate in court proceedings
Debtors seeking loss mitigation counseling, financial literacy education, or mediation services
diagnosis: This profile is for debtors who must file bankruptcy in Eastern New York federal court and need information about the court's systems, locations, and pro se filing options. Critical caveat: this is the actual bankruptcy court, not a law firm or filing service. Pro se bankruptcy filing carries substantial legal risk and should only be undertaken by individuals with strong legal literacy or no financial means to hire an attorney. Debtors without resources should seek free legal aid before attempting pro se filing.
services: Electronic case filing and document management through CM/ECF
Case location and docket access via PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records)
Proof of Claim filing through ePOC (electronic Proof of Claim)
Debtor Electronic Noticing for automatic case notifications
Pro se filing through eSR (Electronic Self-Representation) program for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13
Chapter 7 fee waiver procedures for indigent debtors
Remote hearing registration and appearance via eCourt Appearances with Zoom/Webex
Means testing information and guidance for Chapter 13 cases
Financial literacy and C.A.R.E. (Credit Abuse Resistance Education) Program
Judicial misconduct complaint filing and bankruptcy fraud reporting
